answers.question: What is the Unraid server hardware? (CPU model, RAM, GPU if any, available storage)
answers.context: Multiple features depend on hardware capabilities: faster-whisper model size and latency (GPU vs CPU), Ollama model sizes (8B vs 70B), number of concurrent containers, and whether GPU acceleration is available. The PRD assumes 'modern CPU, 32GB+ RAM, no GPU' as default but this needs confirmation. Affects F07 (Ollama), F10 (faster-whisper), and overall container resource allocation.
answers.selected_answer: Intel Core i7-9700 (8C/8T, 3.0GHz base/4.7GHz turbo), 128GB DDR4 RAM, no dedicated GPU, 32TB array with ~26TB free. HP motherboard (8597). Well-provisioned — 128GB RAM means Ollama 70B models are feasible, multiple Supabase containers are no problem. Only constraint is CPU-only transcription (no GPU). Faster-whisper on int8 CPU will be slower but workable.

answers.question: Which embedding model should be used? nomic-embed-text (768d), mxbai-embed-large (1024d), or cloud text-embedding-3-small (1536d)?
answers.context: This is called out as a NEED CLARIFICATION in the schema definition (line 284) and again in F07. The embedding dimension directly affects the Postgres vector column definition, HNSW index configuration, and the match_captures function signature. The PRD's SQL currently uses vector(1536) but defaults to nomic-embed-text (768d) — these are contradictory. Changing the embedding dimension after data is loaded requires re-embedding all captures. This decision MUST be made before the schema is finalized. Risk item explicitly listed in Section 11.
answers.selected_answer: nomic-embed-text (768d) via Ollama — local, fast, free, good quality. Schema will use vector(768) throughout. Aligns with self-hosted philosophy. 768 dimensions is plenty for personal knowledge search at this scale.

answers.question: What is the preferred method for external access to the MCP server: Tailscale Funnel, Cloudflare Tunnel, or direct port forwarding?
answers.context: The MCP server needs to be accessible from Claude Desktop, Claude Code, and ChatGPT when not on the home LAN. The PRD mentions a URL format of 'https://brain.k4jda.net/mcp?key=<access_key>' but doesn't confirm which tunneling approach. Tailscale Funnel is the default assumption. This also affects whether Slack slash commands (F11) are feasible, since those require Slack to POST to a public URL.
answers.selected_answer: Hybrid approach — existing Tailscale + SWAG reverse proxy stays untouched for all current services, add Cloudflare Tunnel (free tier) only for brain.k4jda.net. Enables public MCP access for Claude Desktop, Claude Code, and ChatGPT, and enables Slack slash commands. Cloudflare Tunnel runs as a lightweight cloudflared Docker container making outbound-only connections. No changes to existing infrastructure.

answers.question: Should Slack voice clips route through the voice-capture container or directly to faster-whisper + core ingest?
answers.context: Slack audio file attachments need transcription. Two architectural paths: (1) route through voice-capture container, which has existing retry/circuit-breaker logic and classification, giving consistent handling for all audio; or (2) send directly to faster-whisper and then core API, which is simpler but duplicates some logic. The PRD's default assumption is to route through voice-capture for consistent classification. This affects F04 and F20 implementation.
answers.selected_answer: Route all audio through voice-capture — add an HTTP endpoint for Slack audio ingestion. Single audio processing path. Reuses existing retry, circuit breaker, classification, and error handling. Consistent metadata extraction regardless of audio source.

answers.question: What is the preferred email delivery method: SMTP via personal email account, a transactional service (Resend/Postmark), or self-hosted mail?
answers.context: F14 requires sending formatted HTML emails for weekly briefs, quarterly reports, and custom skill outputs. SMTP via existing email is simplest for single-user but may have deliverability issues or rate limits. Transactional services cost money but are reliable. Self-hosted is complex. Default assumption is SMTP via existing account.
answers.selected_answer: SMTP via existing email account. Zero cost, zero new accounts, works immediately. For single-user self-sending, deliverability isn't an issue. App password + SMTP config in container env var.

answers.question: What is the current Unraid backup strategy, and how should Postgres data be included?
answers.context: The PRD mentions pg_dump on a schedule as the approach for backing up the Supabase/Postgres data, but needs to know the existing Unraid backup infrastructure. Data loss is rated as 'Critical' impact in the risk assessment. Need to determine: backup frequency, offsite storage location, retention policy, and whether there's an existing Unraid backup plugin or strategy to integrate with.
answers.selected_answer: Daily pg_dump to Unraid share + weekly offsite to Google Drive via rclone (already in the stack). 7-day local retention, 30-day cloud retention. Reuses existing rclone setup — just add a backup folder to the sync config.

answers.question: Should Notion be maintained as a parallel output target during the voice-capture migration, and if so, for how long?
answers.context: voice-capture currently posts to Notion. The migration swaps Notion for Open Brain's core API. Running both in parallel reduces risk but increases complexity. The PRD's default assumption is 'yes, run parallel for 2-4 weeks.' Need confirmation and any specific criteria for when to cut over (e.g., X days with zero failures).
answers.selected_answer: Hard cutover, no parallel operation. Build a one-time Notion backfill script as safety net for recovery if needed. Clean break rather than managing dual outputs.

answers.question: Preferred web UI framework: Next.js (SSR, API routes, PWA) or simpler Vite + React (lighter footprint)?
answers.context: The PRD specifies Next.js 15+ with Tailwind and shadcn/ui, but Section 12 lists this as an open question. Next.js is heavier (more RAM, more complex deployment) but offers SSR, API routes, and better PWA support. Vite + React is lighter on the Unraid server but requires separate API handling. Given this is Phase 4, there's time to decide, but the choice could influence earlier API design decisions.
answers.selected_answer: Vite + React — lightweight SPA calling Core API, with Tailwind + shadcn/ui + vite-plugin-pwa. Core API (Hono) already handles all server-side logic, so Next.js SSR/API routes are redundant. Simpler build, smaller container, faster dev experience.

answers.question: Should the bot use Slack slash commands (/ob) requiring a public URL, or in-channel prefix commands (!stats, ? query) using the Events API?
answers.context: Slack slash commands require Slack to POST to a publicly reachable URL, which means either Tailscale Funnel, Cloudflare Tunnel, or port forwarding must be set up and stable. In-channel prefix commands work entirely through the Events API (outbound websocket), which is simpler and doesn't need public URL exposure. Default assumption is prefix commands. This interacts with the external access decision (question 3).
answers.selected_answer: Both — prefix commands as baseline (Socket Mode, always works), slash commands added as enhancement once Cloudflare Tunnel is stable. Prefix commands work from day one with zero public URL dependency. Slash commands add autocomplete and private responses as a bonus. If tunnel is down, prefix commands still work.

answers.question: How are brain_views assigned to captures, and what are the initial brain view definitions?
answers.context: Brain views are mentioned throughout the PRD (captures table has brain_views column, pipeline routing uses them, search filters by them, config/brain-views.yaml is in the file structure) but there's no definition of what the initial brain views are, how they're assigned (manual tags? auto-classified? both?), or what pipeline routing rules differ per view. The career pipeline extends default with extract_career_signals, suggesting brain_view-based routing, but the rules aren't specified.
answers.selected_answer: Five brain views: career (strategic trajectory, governance, bets), personal (non-work: physics, ham radio, sailing, electronics, life), technical (hands-on building, coding, architecture, tooling), work-internal (Stratfield operations: proposals, pipeline, pricing, partnerships, BD), client (client-specific: engagements, deliverables, stakeholder notes). Hybrid assignment — auto-classified by metadata extraction stage, overridable via explicit tags. Multi-view assignment supported (a capture can belong to multiple views). Pipeline routing: career triggers extended pipeline with extract_career_signals, client triggers extended pipeline with extract_client_signals, others use default pipeline.

answers.question: What is the fallback behavior when the LLM-based intent router fails or is unavailable?
answers.context: The PRD states the intent router should 'correctly distinguish capture vs. query >95% of the time' and uses Ollama llama3.1:8b with 'no fallback — degrade gracefully with prefix-only detection.' But the actual prefix-only rules aren't fully specified. Need to define: which prefixes map to which intents, what happens with ambiguous messages (no prefix, no clear question pattern), and whether the default-to-capture assumption is always correct.
answers.selected_answer: Prefix-only fallback with default-to-capture when LLM unavailable. Prefix rules: '?' = query, '!' = command, '@Open Brain' = query, no prefix = capture. Default-to-capture ensures no data loss (false capture is recoverable, missed capture is not). Intent classification model configurable via AI router — initial model TBD based on available hardware (Jetson Orin Nano, DGX Spark, or Unraid Ollama). Model choice is a config change, not an architecture decision.

answers.question: What should the initial prompt templates contain for metadata extraction, career signal extraction, intent routing, and synthesis?
answers.context: The PRD references 7 prompt template files (extract_metadata_v1.txt, career_signals_v1.txt, weekly_brief_v1.txt, board_quick_check_v1.txt, board_quarterly_v1.txt, synthesis_v1.txt, intent_router_v1.txt) but provides no content for any of them. These are critical for AI processing quality. The metadata extraction prompt needs to specify which fields to extract (people, topics, type, action_items, dates), what output format to use (JSON?), and how to handle ambiguous content.
answers.selected_answer: Define metadata extraction and intent router prompts for Phase 1 (the two needed immediately), defer remaining 5 to their respective phases. Prompts are versioned text files in config/prompts/ (e.g., extract_metadata_v1.txt, v2, v3), hot-reloadable without container restart, iterable based on real capture data. Output format: structured JSON. Include the 5 brain views and 8 capture types in the extraction prompt. Add new prompts as needed (e.g., extract_client_signals_v1.txt for client brain view).

answers.question: What is the detailed state machine design for the governance session FSM, including states, transitions, and anti-vagueness gate logic?
answers.context: The PRD describes governance sessions at a feature level (5-question audit, anti-vagueness enforcement, max 2 skips) but doesn't define the FSM states, transitions, or how the anti-vagueness gate evaluates answer quality. board-journal presumably has this logic — need to either port the exact FSM or redesign it. Key unknowns: what constitutes a 'vague' answer, how is 'concrete evidence' evaluated, what happens after 2 skips, and how does the session generate its 90-day prediction.
answers.selected_answer: Redesign governance for Slack-native conversational interaction rather than porting board-journal's rigid FSM. Preserve board-journal's core principles: 5-area coverage, anti-vagueness enforcement, mandatory bet creation, evidence-based assessment. Use board-journal as reference for *what to enforce*, not *how to enforce it*. The bot reads context, asks follow-ups naturally, pushes back conversationally rather than via rigid state transitions. Lighter FSM with more AI-driven conversation management and guardrails. Detailed design in Phase 3 with board-journal code as conceptual reference.

answers.question: What are the detailed personality definitions, system prompts, and interaction patterns for each of the 7 board roles?
answers.context: The PRD lists 7 board roles (Accountability, Market Reality, Avoidance, Long-term Positioning, Devil's Advocate, Portfolio Defender, Opportunity Scout) with one-sentence purposes but no personality definitions, system prompts, or behavioral guidelines. The quarterly review mentions 'board roles interrogate the evidence' — need to define how each role's perspective is implemented in AI prompts, how they interact with each other, and what triggers the 2 optional growth roles.
answers.selected_answer: Defer role personality definitions to Phase 3. Use board-journal prompts as starting reference, redesign voices for Slack-native conversational flow. Role perspectives stay the same but voice and interaction patterns designed alongside the conversational governance flow, not in isolation.

answers.question: What monitoring and observability approach should be used for the system?
answers.context: The PRD specifies health checks, pipeline logging, and token usage tracking, but doesn't address centralized log aggregation, alerting beyond Pushover notifications for specific events, container resource monitoring, or how to debug issues across multiple containers. For a system with 10+ Docker containers, some observability strategy is needed — even if it's just Docker logs + Unraid's built-in monitoring.
answers.selected_answer: Lean monitoring — Docker logs + Unraid dashboard + pipeline_log/skills_log tables in Postgres + Pushover alerts for failures. No dedicated monitoring stack (no Prometheus/Grafana/Loki). Dockhand (dockhand.pro) under consideration for future container management and may cover additional observability needs when deployed.

answers.question: What is the database schema migration strategy as the system evolves across phases?
answers.context: Phase 1 creates core tables (captures), Phase 3 adds entities and sessions tables, Phase 4 may add more. Need a migration strategy — will Supabase's built-in migration tooling be used, a migration library like drizzle-kit or prisma migrate, or raw SQL scripts? This affects how schema changes are versioned and applied without data loss.
answers.selected_answer: Drizzle ORM + drizzle-kit migrations — TypeScript-native, schema-as-code, auto-generated SQL migrations. Type-safe database queries as a bonus. Fits the Hono/TS stack. Zero runtime overhead — generates raw SQL.

answers.question: Are there token/cost budget limits for cloud AI providers, and what happens when limits are reached?
answers.context: The AI router logs token usage and cost estimates, but there's no mention of budget caps, daily/monthly spending limits, or what happens when costs exceed expectations. With governance sessions using Claude Opus (expensive) and synthesis using Claude Sonnet, costs could add up. Should the system have configurable spending limits that trigger fallback to cheaper models or local-only processing?
answers.selected_answer: Configurable monthly budget cap with soft and hard limits. Soft limit at $30/month triggers Pushover alert. Hard limit at $50/month triggers circuit breaker — falls back to local models only. Budget tracked via existing token usage logging in skills_log table. Normal expected usage ~$15-30/month.

answers.question: What are the detailed specifications for the /api/v1/synthesize endpoint — input parameters, context window management, and output format?
answers.context: The synthesize endpoint is mentioned as distinct from search (search returns raw captures, synthesize feeds captures to an AI model for a coherent response), but the API spec doesn't detail its parameters. Key unknowns: how many captures are fed as context (all matching? top N?), what happens if context exceeds the model's token limit, what the output format looks like, and whether synthesis results are cached or captured back into the brain.
answers.selected_answer: Top-N retrieval with configurable token budget for the /api/v1/synthesize endpoint. Default top 20 captures, 50K token budget. If context exceeds budget, truncate from bottom (lowest similarity). Ad-hoc synthesis results are ephemeral (not cached, not re-captured into the brain). Output skills (weekly brief, governance) implement their own context assembly strategies — they call /api/v1/search for captures and handle chunking/summarization internally before calling the AI router directly. This separates the simple ad-hoc endpoint from the sophisticated skill-level context management needed for large-scale synthesis.

answers.question: What are the container resource limits (CPU, memory) for each Docker container, and what is the expected total resource footprint?
answers.context: The system architecture shows 10+ containers running simultaneously on Unraid: Supabase stack (Postgres, PostgREST, Studio, GoTrue, Realtime — 5+ containers alone), Redis, Ollama, faster-whisper, Core API, Slack bot, MCP server, Workers, voice-capture, rclone, and eventually Web UI. Without resource limits, containers could starve each other. Need at minimum: memory limits per container and an estimate of total RAM/CPU required.
answers.selected_answer: Memory limits on heavy hitters only — Ollama: 16GB, faster-whisper: 8GB, Postgres: 8GB. Lightweight containers (Core API, Slack bot, MCP server, Workers, voice-capture, Redis, rclone, etc.) unconstrained. Total estimated footprint ~20-25GB of 128GB available. Ample headroom for Unraid itself, other containers, and file caching.

answers.question: What is the full taxonomy of capture types, and how does type affect pipeline processing and display?
answers.context: The PRD references capture types in several places (decision, idea, observation, task, win, task-completed) but doesn't provide a complete taxonomy or define how type is determined. Is type extracted by the metadata extraction stage? Can a user explicitly set it? Does type affect which pipeline stages run or how the capture appears in search results and the weekly brief? The weekly brief references 'captures typed as win/decision/task-completed' for the Wins section.
answers.selected_answer: Eight core types: decision, idea, observation, task, win, blocker, question, reflection. Auto-classified by metadata extraction stage, manually overridable via explicit prefix (e.g., 'Decision: ...'). Type affects display (Slack search results, timeline) and weekly brief grouping (win/decision → Wins section, blocker → Blockers, task → Open Loops, question → unresolved items). Type does NOT affect pipeline routing (brain_views handle that). Taxonomy is extensible — add new types by updating the extraction prompt template, no schema or code changes required.

answers.question: How should YAML configuration changes be detected and applied without container restarts?
answers.context: Both the pipeline (F03) and AI router (F08) specify that 'configuration changes take effect without restart.' The PRD mentions 'file watch or API reload' as options. Need to decide: file system watching (fsnotify/chokidar), API endpoint to trigger reload, or both. Also need to consider what happens to in-flight pipeline jobs when config changes — do they use the old config or switch mid-processing?
answers.selected_answer: Re-read config on each pipeline job start — naturally picks up changes within seconds. API reload endpoint (POST /admin/reload-config) for immediate cache clear when needed. In-flight jobs complete with the config they started with — consistent, predictable, no mid-job surprises. No file-system watcher needed. At <50 captures/day, re-reading a small YAML file per job has zero performance impact.

answers.question: How should the bot handle thread-based follow-up interactions when Slack free plan loses message history after 90 days?
answers.context: The query system uses Slack threads for follow-up (reply with a number for detail, 'more' for pagination, refined queries). The Slack free plan drops messages after 90 days. While captures are safe in Supabase, the bot needs to track thread state (what search results were shown, what page the user is on). Need to define: where is thread state stored (Redis? Postgres?), how long is it retained, and what happens if a user replies to an old thread?
answers.selected_answer: Redis with 1-hour TTL — store thread context keyed by Slack thread_ts, auto-expire. Thread context is tiny (~1KB per thread: result IDs, current page, query text). If user replies to an expired thread, bot responds with 'That thread has expired — start a new search.' Zero maintenance, no database clutter.

answers.question: How does the quarterly review session support pausing and resuming across multiple sittings?
answers.context: The PRD states quarterly reviews can span 'potentially multiple sittings' and that sessions can be 'paused and resumed (session state persisted).' The sessions table has state (jsonb) and status fields. But the UX flow isn't defined: how does the user resume (type a command? bot prompts them?), how long can a session stay paused, and what happens to the session's AI context window across sittings (LLM context can't persist across API calls).
answers.selected_answer: Session state in Postgres (sessions table) + transcript replay on resume. User resumes via Slack command (!board resume) or by replying in the original thread. On resume, load transcript and feed to AI as context — model picks up where it left off. Max pause duration: 30 days, then session auto-expires. Slack threads persist for 90 days on free plan, so original thread is accessible.

answers.question: What is the complete deduplication strategy across all input sources?
answers.context: The PRD mentions Slack deduplication via slack_ts and voice-capture deduplication via filename, plus pipeline idempotency. But what about: MCP capture_thought deduplication (user accidentally submits same thought twice), web dashboard voice capture deduplication, or near-duplicate detection (same thought captured via both Slack and voice memo)? Also, Slack retries can send the same event multiple times — the idempotency note covers this but the implementation isn't specified.
answers.selected_answer: Source-level dedup only — Slack via slack_ts, voice-capture via filename, MCP via content hash + 60-second window. No cross-source near-duplicate detection. Cross-source duplicates (same thought via Slack and voice) are rare and arguably valuable to keep both — voice version may have nuances text version doesn't. Semantic dedup at ingest would add 1-2 seconds latency for a problem that almost never occurs.

answers.question: What Docker network topology should be used, and which containers need to communicate with which?
answers.context: With 10+ containers, the Docker networking setup matters. Need to define: a single Docker network vs. multiple isolated networks, which containers expose ports to the host vs. only communicating internally, and DNS resolution between containers. The architecture diagram shows communication flows but doesn't specify network topology. Supabase self-hosted also has its own internal networking requirements.
answers.selected_answer: Two networks — supabase-internal for Supabase components (Studio, Realtime, etc.), open-brain for everything else (Core API, Slack bot, MCP, Workers, Ollama, faster-whisper, Redis, voice-capture). Postgres bridges both networks. Only Slack bot, MCP server, and Web UI expose host ports. All Open Brain containers use simple DNS resolution (http://ollama:11434, http://redis:6379).

answers.question: How should the system handle embedding model changes or fallback embeddings from different models?
answers.context: The AI router has an embedding fallback from Ollama nomic-embed-text to OpenAI text-embedding-3-small. These produce different-dimension vectors (768 vs 1536). Mixing embedding dimensions in the same vector column would break similarity search. Need a strategy: either don't fall back for embeddings (accept failure), pad/truncate dimensions (degrades quality), or maintain separate vector columns per model.
answers.selected_answer: No embedding fallback. If Ollama is down, queue captures and retry when it recovers. All embeddings must come from the same model (nomic-embed-text) for search consistency. Mixing models degrades quality even when dimensions match — different models place concepts in different vector spaces. Pipeline retry logic handles transient Ollama failures. On 128GB server, Ollama should have near-100% uptime.

answers.question: What is the detailed retry and error handling strategy for pipeline stages, including backoff parameters and dead-letter behavior?
answers.context: The PRD mentions 'retry with exponential backoff (configurable max retries)' and the API has a retry endpoint. But specifics are missing: initial retry delay, backoff multiplier, max retries per stage (the non-functional requirements say 3), what happens after all retries are exhausted (dead-letter queue? permanent failure status?), and how failed captures surface for manual intervention beyond the Pushover notification.
answers.selected_answer: 5 retries per stage with patient exponential backoff (30s, 2m, 10m, 30m, 2h), then mark stage as failed. Pushover alert on final failure. Daily auto-retry sweep for all failed stages (one additional attempt per day). Manual retry also available via API (POST /captures/:id/retry?stage=...). Persistent failures that fail the daily sweep stay marked as failed for manual investigation via !pipeline status Slack command.

answers.question: How should the system handle the gap between when a thought occurred (captured_at) and when it enters the system (created_at), especially for voice memos?
answers.context: The captures table has both captured_at and created_at. For Slack messages, these are nearly identical. For voice memos, there could be a 3-5+ minute gap (Apple Watch → Google Drive → rclone sync → processing). The ingest API payload doesn't include a captured_at field — who sets it? Does voice-capture pass the audio file's creation timestamp? How does this affect the weekly brief's date grouping and timeline views?
answers.selected_answer: Add captured_at to the ingest API payload as an optional field. Input adapters pass original timestamp — voice-capture passes audio file creation timestamp, Slack bot passes slack_ts. If not provided, defaults to created_at. All time-based queries, displays, weekly brief grouping, and timeline views use captured_at. The brain's timeline reflects when you had thoughts, not when the system processed them.

answers.question: What specific features from board-journal need to be ported, and is the existing codebase available as reference?
answers.context: The PRD says Open Brain 'replaces board-journal' and inherits governance sessions, weekly briefs, and bet tracking. Phase 3 ports the governance FSM. But it's unclear which board-journal features are being carried forward vs. reimagined, whether the Flutter app's prompt engineering and session logic are documented or only in code, and whether the board-journal codebase is accessible for reference during implementation.
answers.selected_answer: Create a conceptual reference document from board-journal — no code, no FSM diagrams, no prompt copy-paste. Capture: governance philosophy, the 5-question audit framework, anti-vagueness principles (what makes an answer 'vague' vs. 'concrete'), board role perspectives and what each challenges, bet tracking model (falsifiable criteria, evaluation triggers, resolution rules), weekly brief structure and what makes it useful, drift detection logic. This becomes the design brief for Phase 3's clean-room implementation with a modern, debt-free architecture. No code-level logic or code itself will be incorporated — concepts and principles only.

answers.question: Which Supabase self-hosted components are actually needed, and can unnecessary ones be excluded to reduce resource usage?
answers.context: Supabase self-hosted includes many components: Postgres, PostgREST, GoTrue (auth), Realtime, Storage, Kong (API gateway), Studio, and more. For a single-user system with no auth needs, many of these aren't required. The Core API uses Hono and talks to Postgres directly — do we even need PostgREST? Studio is useful for debugging but GoTrue/Kong/Realtime may be unnecessary overhead. Trimming the stack reduces the container count and resource usage significantly.
answers.selected_answer: Postgres + pgvector + Studio + Realtime. Skip PostgREST, GoTrue, Kong, Storage. Deploy everything needed through Phase 4 at once (Realtime for web dashboard live updates) while trimming components that are genuinely unnecessary. Core API handles all data access via Hono + Drizzle — PostgREST is redundant. No auth needed — GoTrue unnecessary. No file storage via Supabase — Storage unnecessary. Kong only routes to PostgREST/GoTrue — unnecessary without them.
